# Analyzing Search Algorithms

# Table and Figures

**Table 1**  
*Path Results of Breadth-First Search (BFS) and A-Star (A\*)*

| Environment No. | Search Settings | Length | Time, in ms | No. of Operations |
| :---: | ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | BFS, no options | 10 | 0.2000 | 443 |
| 1 | BFS, with bi-directional | 10 | 0.3000 | 203 |
| 1 | BFS, with allow diagonal | 10 | 1.1000 | 843 |
| 1 | BFS, with allow diagonal & bi-directional | 10 | 0.4000 | 361 |
| 1 | A\*, Manhattan | 10 | 1.0000 | 43 |
| 1 | A\*, Euclidean 7 | 10 | 0.2000 | 43 |
| 1 | A\*, Manhattan with bi-directional | 10 | 0.2000 | 43 |
| 1 | A\*, Manhattan with diagonal | 10 | 0.6000 | 47 |
| 1 | A\*, Manhattan with diagonal & bi-directional | 10 | 0.2000 | 49 |
| 1 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with bi-directional | 10 | 1.0000 | 43 |
| 1 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with diagonal | 10 | 1.0000 | 47 |
| 1 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with diagonal & bi-directional | 10 | 0.4000 | 49 |
| 2 | BFS, no options | 13 | 0.3000 | 731 |
| 2 | BFS, with bi-directional | 13 | 0.1000 | 299 |
| 2 | BFS, with allow diagonal | 9.49 | 0.3000 | 449 |
| 2 | BFS, with allow diagonal & bi-directional | 10.07 | 0.2000 | 185 |
| 2 | A\*, Manhattan | 13 | 0.2000 | 93 |
| 2 | A\*, Euclidean 7 | 13 | 0.3000 | 147 |
| 2 | A\*, Manhattan with bi-directional | 13 | 1.1000 | 81 |
| 2 | A\*, Manhattan with diagonal | 9.49 | 0.3000 | 47 |
| 2 | A\*, Manhattan with diagonal & bi-directional | 9.49 | 0.3000 | 45 |
| 2 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with bi-directional | 13 | 0.3000 | 107 |
| 2 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with diagonal | 9.49 | 0.3000 | 53 |
| 2 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with diagonal & bi-directional | 9.49 | 0.2000 | 45 |
| 3 | BFS, no options | 19 | 0.1000 | 294 |
| 3 | BFS, with bi-directional | 19 | 0.2000 | 247 |
| 3 | BFS, with allow diagonal | 15.49 | 0.3000 | 287 |
| 3 | BFS, with allow diagonal & bi-directional | 15.49 | 0.1000 | 219 |
| 3 | A\*, Manhattan | 19 | 0.4000 | 212 |
| 3 | A\*, Euclidean 7 | 19 | 0.2000 | 218 |
| 3 | A\*, Manhattan with bi-directional | 19 | 0.2000 | 118 |
| 3 | A\*, Manhattan with diagonal | 15.49 | 0.4000 | 191 |
| 3 | A\*, Manhattan with diagonal & bi-directional | 15.49 | 0.3000 | 92 |
| 3 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with bi-directional | 19 | 1.2000 | 116 |
| 3 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with diagonal | 15.49 | 0.4000 | 198 |
| 3 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with diagonal & bi-directional | 15.49 | 0.5000 | 108 |
| 4 | BFS, no options | 30 | 0.3000 | 235 |
| 4 | BFS, with bi-directional | 30 | 0.2000 | 193 |
| 4 | BFS, with allow diagonal | 25.9 | 0.2000 | 250 |
| 4 | BFS, with allow diagonal & bi-directional | 25.9 | 0.3000 | 219 |
| 4 | A\*, Manhattan | 30 | 0.2000 | 167 |
| 4 | A\*, Euclidean 7 | 30 | 0.2000 | 166 |
| 4 | A\*, Manhattan with bi-directional | 30 | 0.3000 | 194 |
| 4 | A\*, Manhattan with diagonal | 25.9 | 0.3000 | 164 |
| 4 | A\*, Manhattan with diagonal & bi-directional | 25.9 | 0.5000 | 211 |
| 4 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with bi-directional | 30 | 0.2000 | 222 |
| 4 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with diagonal | 25.9 | 0.3000 | 170 |
| 4 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with diagonal & bi-directional | 25.9 | 0.4000 | 233 |
| 5 | BFS, no options | 70 | 0.2000 | 218 |
| 5 | BFS, with bi-directional | 70 | 0.3000 | 215 |
| 5 | BFS, with allow diagonal | 59.7 | 0.2000 | 218 |
| 5 | BFS, with allow diagonal & bi-directional | 59.7 | 0.3000 | 202 |
| 5 | A\*, Manhattan | 70 | 0.3000 | 218 |
| 5 | A\*, Euclidean 7 | 70 | 0.3000 | 218 |
| 5 | A\*, Manhattan with bi-directional | 72 | 0.4000 | 207 |
| 5 | A\*, Manhattan with diagonal | 59.7 | 0.3000 | 216 |
| 5 | A\*, Manhattan with diagonal & bi-directional | 59.7 | 0.3000 | 204 |
| 5 | A\*, Euclidean 7 with bi-directional | 72 | 0.2000 | 215 |

# Analysis

## **Overall, how did these search settings perform. How do you define the best algorithm?**

All search settings performed better with bi-directional traversal, and BFS notably benefited the most from bi-directional. Of all search types, A\* Manhattan with both diagonal and bi-directional performed the best. 

In these grid environments, search algorithms are used to determine the nearest path from green to red. By comparing the total length of paths, as well as total number of operations across the environments and by search setting, I determined that the best outcomes are those that performed the least amount of operations to find the shortest possible route. Allowing diagonal path traversal accounted for finding the shortest overall paths, and bi-directional searches usually lowered the number of operations. The time did not appear to correlate with any clear pattern or consistency and were not tested through multiple trials, so I did not use their numbers in my analysis.

# **What environmental factors impacted the performance of each algorithm the most?**

In A\* search, narrow walls and multiple linear paths in Environments 4 and 5 saw the least benefit or penalty from diagonal use. While A\* performed better overall in Environment 4, its use of bi-directional searches increased the number of operations.

Environment 1 and 2’s endpoint placement had a direct impact on BFS use of diagonal search. Diagonals took significantly longer (number of operations) to find the straight-line route to the endpoint in 1, whereas the diagonal route to the endpoint in 2 was found in less than half the operations than with BFS alone.

Environment 5 was structured with two narrow and linear paths of similar length which resulted in little variation in the number of operations. The path length was always reduced with diagonals enabled, and all searches saw benefit when combined with bi-directional. When set to bi-directional and no other options though, both A\* searches chose the slightly longer path of 72-length.

## **Analyzing these tools as an application of artificial intelligence**

While each search setting takes different steps and methods to move and explore the environment, it still provides what is the optimal path based on what it perceives to be a priority. The program itself knows where the point is, but it is limited by the movement and obstacles in its environment. A\* Manhattan for instance, will take available branches and hug walls as it attempts to bee-line as straight as possible to the endpoint. A\* Euclidean is similar, but allows itself to explore more of the available open space in the event of a clearer path. BFS may explore more thoroughly in a radius, but it can still determine best path once the environment is known.
